abstract: Our understanding of secondary organic aerosol (SOA) formation within biomass burning plumes remains constrained by the incomplete identification and quantification of emitted non-methane organic compounds (NMOCs). To address this gap, we collected gaseous organic compounds on sorbent cartridges during laboratory burns as part of the fourth Fire Lab at Missoula Experiment (FLAME-4). These samples were analyzed using two-dimensional gas chromatography–time-of-flight mass spectrometry (GC × GC–ToFMS). Leveraging the high sensitivity and superior resolving power of this technique, we generated the most comprehensive biomass burning NMOC dataset to date, featuring measurements for 708 positively or tentatively identified species. We present calculated emission factors (EFs) for these compounds across six distinct fuel types, including conifer branches, grasses, agricultural residues, and peat. The number of compounds meeting our selection criteria ranged from 129 to 474 per burn and included numerous complex isomer groups. For instance, we identified 38 monoterpene isomers in emissions from coniferous fuels; the observed isomeric ratios align closely with those found in corresponding essential oils, suggesting that oil composition is a reliable predictor for fuel-dependent terpene emissions. Additionally, we detected and tentatively identified 11 sesquiterpenes, representing the first reported speciation of these compounds in gas-phase biomass burning emissions. Finally, we evaluate the calculated EFs for all measured compounds within the context of their potential contribution to SOA formation.
